title: Module 3 Quiz
description: Test your understanding of visualization, highlight reels, disaster planning, self-talk, and mindfulness.

blocks.content: Seven questions covering Module 3: visualization, highlight reels, disaster planning, self-talk, and mindfulness. Retrieval practice — one of the most effective ways to lock knowledge into long-term memory.

blocks.question: 1. Process imagery beats outcome imagery because:
blocks.explanation: Correct. Visualizing the finish line feels great but has near-zero motor rehearsal value. Process imagery — form, breathing, execution mechanics — activates the same neural architecture as physical practice.

options.text: Outcome imagery is too difficult to perform vividly
Process imagery rehearses HOW you execute, activating motor circuits that mirror actual performance
Outcome imagery causes anxiety
Process imagery builds emotional resilience rather than motor execution patterns

blocks.question: 2. PETTLEP: Physical, ___, Task, Timing, ___, Emotion, Perspective.
blocks.explanation: Correct. Physical, Environment, Task, Timing, Learning, Emotion, Perspective. Environment means the actual venue. Learning means updating imagery as your skill develops.

options.text: Endurance, Listening
Environment, Learning
Energy, Location
Effort, Longevity

blocks.question: 3. The Highlight Reel's three parts are:
blocks.explanation: Correct. Past anchors confidence in evidence. Future programs the decisive moment. Upcoming bridges rehearsal into full execution.

options.text: Warm-up, race, cool-down
Physical, mental, emotional preparation
Empowering past memory, crucial future moment, upcoming event vision
Instructional cue, motivational phrase, breathing exercise

blocks.question: 4. Sort the following into Instructional vs. Motivational self-talk: 'Light feet, quick turnover' / 'You've been here before' / 'Shoulders down, hands loose' / 'Stay in it — this is your race'
blocks.explanation: Correct. Instructional cues direct technique ('Light feet,' 'Shoulders down'). Motivational phrases regulate emotion and drive ('You've been here before,' 'Stay in it').

options.text: Instructional: 'Light feet' and 'Shoulders down' — Motivational: 'You've been here before' and 'Stay in it'
Instructional: 'You've been here before' and 'Stay in it' — Motivational: 'Light feet' and 'Shoulders down'
All four are motivational
All four are instructional

blocks.question: 5. Match the mindfulness facet to its definition: The ability to let thoughts come and go without needing to respond is called:
blocks.explanation: Correct. Non-Reacting is letting thoughts and feelings pass without engaging. The thought knocks — you don't answer the door.

options.text: Observing
Describing
Non-Judging
Non-Reacting

blocks.question: 6. An effective If-Then plan must be:
blocks.explanation: Correct. 'If [specific trigger], then I will [specific action].' Specificity converts deliberation into automation — a System 2 decision pre-loaded into System 1.

options.text: Positive and optimistic in tone
Specific in both the trigger and the response
Kept only in your head so it stays flexible
Focused on preventing the problem from occurring

blocks.question: 7. You're at mile 70 of a 100-mile race. Your legs cramp on a climb and negative self-talk spirals: 'I'm done. I can't finish this.' Which response best deploys the tools from this module?
blocks.explanation: Correct. Pre-loaded If-Then plan handles the cramp. Trained self-talk answers the spiral. Present-moment focus prevents the chimp from hijacking the next 30 miles. Tools deployed, not improvised.

options.text: Push through the pain — mental toughness means ignoring everything
Drop out at the next aid station — cramping means your body is done
Fire your If-Then plan (walk the uphill, increase sodium), deploy your motivational self-talk ('You've been here before'), and use a 60-second breathing focus to stay present
Visualize the finish line to motivate yourself
